segments.text: Recording is paused.
Rachel, you're, you're, you're safe. I was
Wondering if he was hiding around the corner making
Public session and back
to our submissions on the Fast Track
approvals Amendment Bill.
And we welcome the Parliamentary Commissioner
for the environment, Simon, to the committee this morning.
Uh, this afternoon, actually this afternoon.
Uh, and you've got 20 minutes with us.
Uh, take your submission as read, uh,
and we'll open up to you for your opening remarks.
Well, thank you Madam Chair, and,
and I, I do hope you have had time to read it,
and I appreciate that you haven't had a lot of time.
Yes, it is, um, better crafted than what I'm going to say
to you, but it would take much longer if I tried to read it.
And I want to leave you time for questions. Obviously.
Look, I've got nothing to offer the committee on the
provisions that relate to supermarkets
or some of the minor technical amendments
that have received broad support.
My comments are confined to the radical proposals that seek
to re-engineer the fast track process in midstream.
As you'll be aware, I hope from my written submission,
I consider that the existing fast track process is working
well, better frankly, than I expected.
That's due to two things principally.
Firstly, the wise decision to leave critical judgements
and determinations to independent properly qualified panels,
empowered to inform themselves about the key
dimensions of what's proposed.
And secondly, the very professional way in which the EPA
and the panel conveners have gone about establishing a
process that is both, uh, transparent and swift
and gives individual cases the best chance
of being properly dealt with.
This bill torpedoes these two positive elements
below the waterline in the short time available.
I won't dwell on the practical process problems the bill
raises, although my submission refers to them,
they're expertly canvassed by the panel conveners
who I think are following me.
These are people of real substance and experience,
and if they tell you something as amiss,
I suggest you listen
because restricting timeframes could actually backfire
and undermine the whole process.
I want instead to use the very limited time available to me
to focus on the two most egregious, substantive provisions,
and they, in my view, are clause five, dealing
with government policy statements
and Clause 33, dealing with input by affected persons.
Now, the proposal in clause five
that ministers should be able
to concoct bespoke government policy statements,
which panels must consider, is a thinly veiled attempt
to guarantee a favorable decision.
The government is asking parliament,
government's asking you Parliament
to give it an open-ended power to determine
what national regional benefit will mean
for any particular type of infrastructure or development.
And in the absence of any transitional provisions,
these policy statements can be applied to applications
that are already in train.
They've already got panels hearing them.
This is executive overreach for which no case has been made.
It is as open-ended
as the infamous Economic Stabilization Act
that allowed governments to regulate
absolutely anything if they thought it
threatened economic stability.
I've really taken aback
that the government should be asking parliament to license
ministers to issue what the New Zealand initiative
charitably, I think describes
as opaque ministerial directions.
There are no guardrails if you enact this
provision, no guardrails.
Ministers will be able to design bespoke policy statements
for particular types of projects in an attempt
to provide certainty for specific applications.
The vague drafting of Clause five means
that ministers could, for example, instruct panelists that
as a matter of government policy mining is
of significant regional benefit when proposed in the Corra
Mandel or on Great Barrier Island.
And that in weighing the benefits of any project,
quantifiable economic benefits must be given greater weight
than less easily quantified intangible environmental cost
or risk, which will pass to future generations.
There is nothing to prevent something as directive as that.
That's the key point about the legislation
that's been put in front of you.
The amendments appear intended to whittle away the autonomy
of panel conveners
and expert panels by making the decision making boundaries
as narrow as possible in an attempt to orbit guarantee
approval for projects that ministers deemed
to be good for us.
Which begs the question, why have a panel at all
if you can even find members to appoint?
And I think it will be hard to get panel members
to serve if you pass this into law.
And also it creates a very strong incentive
to lobby ministers for special favors with all the risks
that entails mm-hmm.
Definitions which contribute to the decision making criteria
that a panel must consider should be prescribed
by you, by parliament.
And some submitters have offered views on
how this could be achieved if the committee thinks
that definition is necessary.
While I'm on the topic of ministerial powers,
you'll note in my submission a discussion of new powers
to direct the EPA on the performance of its functions
and duties and the exercise of its powers.
I encourage the committee to query this with officials
and in particular, satisfy yourself that Clause 48
can't be used as a mechanism
for directing panels and conveners.
Now let me turn to clause 33, which replaces the ability
of the panel to invite comments from
any other person the panel considers appropriate
with a limitation that the panel can only invite comments
from other persons if the relevant local authorities
or agencies do not intend to comment on the matter
or if the comments they intend
to make will not sufficiently address the
matter how I won.
Are panels supposed
to know whether local authorities are going to
sufficiently address a matter?
More importantly, since when
have local authorities become the only legitimate
mouthpieces for public concern as a rate payer,
I don't think they're,
it will certainly place enormous pressure on local
authorities as the only voice for community
and wider public concerns.
Now I have, uh, with me here today,
team members Shannon Watson, who I recruited specifically
because of his practical experience and consenting matters.
He's actually been involved in some fast track stuff.
If you've got questions about
what impact the additional pressures imposed
by this bill might have on overstretched local authorities,
he may be able to help you.
The bill seeks to significantly reduce the ability
of people whose property
and lives could be placed at risk
to even express their views to panels.
Some of you who are on the committee
that heard submissions on the original bill,
which is now law, may recall that I mentioned the air case.
You may recall that the air case
and the oral submission I made to you air was an application
for a very large
above ground water storage dam in North Canterbury.
Uh, issues in the early stage of the hearing were
of a highly technical engineering nature,
but they suddenly became real
because during a a a break in the hearings,
the kaikoura earthquake happened.
Now it emerged that in the event of a dam breach,
about 200 homes were located in
what would be the flow path
of fast moving flood waters stretching for some kilometers
below the site and located along small unsealed rural roads.
Now, I expressed concern about those people, uh,
having the ability to, uh, actually put something
to a panel before.
But these are the sort
of people a panel would under this bill no longer be able
to seek comment from panel, could not seek
comment from those people.
Now, I think committee members should ask themselves
if they would want to live below a tailings dam
or a reservoir which could fail,
but have no ability to contribute their views
to a process in which the government has
decreed that there are benefits.
And where a decision is being made under punishing
time constraints.
It seems to me that something like a tailings dam will
inevitably impact on property values downstream
of that structure.
If that's the case, then the decision
to fast track a mining consent favors one person's property
rights at the expense of another without giving
the latter any chance to have their say.
Finally, there has been no serious
regulatory analysis of this bill,
and I strongly suggest
that the committee seeks detailed information from officials
in the Ministry for Regulation
or the Minister to explain the absence of a policy rationale
or regulatory impact analysis.
That's enough from me.
I think we have a little time for questions. Thank
You. Uh, thank

It's working as was intended.
The purpose of the Fast Track Approvals Act is
to facilitate the delivery of infrastructure
and development projects that provide significant regional
and national benefits.
The legislation responds to broad cross party recognition
of New Zealand's infrastructure deficit
and the need to support sustainable economic growth.
And the key features of your existing act are,
which facilitates infrastructure
and development, are
that the act is a one-stop shop for approvals.
Applications are not notified the timeframe

By the panel convener.

Only the acts inquiry led approach

The work of the panel convenience has been on process

Thursday, 20th November 2025 | Environment Committee

Fast-track Approvals Amendment Bill

Hearing of evidence:

Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment
Simon Upton, Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment
Rebecca Thornley, General Council

Panel Convenor for the Fast Track Approvals Act 2024
Jane Borthwick, Panel Convenor
Jennifer Caldwell, Associate Convenor
